prompt_md: A class `Distance` has a private field `m_meters` and a public accessor `double meters() const`. Which approach lets you write `operator+` as a normal (non-friend) free function?

options.a: Access `m_meters` directly — private fields are visible to free functions in the same file
options.b: Use the public `meters()` accessor to read the value and construct the result
options.c: Declare `operator+` inside the class body without `friend`
options.d: Add `static` to the function signature so it can bypass access control

explanation_md: A normal free function can only access `public` members. Since `meters()` is public, it provides exactly the data needed. `m_meters` is private and inaccessible without a `friend` declaration. `static` has no effect on access control.
`operator+` uses the public `val()` accessor to read 4 and 7, sums them to 11, and returns `Kg{11}`. No friend access is needed because `val()` is public.

options.a: Binary operators must be defined inside the class body as member functions
options.b: `operator+` is a normal free function that tries to access private `m_deg` — this requires either a public accessor or a `friend` declaration
options.c: The constructor parameter must be `int`, not `double`
options.d: `Angle` objects cannot be returned by value from a free function

explanation_md: `m_deg` is private. A normal free function has no access to private members. The fix is either to add a public `double deg() const { return m_deg; }` accessor and use it in the operator, or to add `friend Angle operator+(const Angle&, const Angle&);` inside the `Angle` class.

prompt_md: When should you prefer a normal free function over a friend function for an overloaded operator?

options.a: Always — friend functions are deprecated in modern C++
options.b: When the class already provides public accessors that expose all the data the operator needs
options.c: When you need the operator to modify one of its operands
options.d: Only when both operands are the same type

explanation_md: If the class exposes the required data through public accessors, a normal free function works without any coupling to the class's private implementation. Friend functions are appropriate when no suitable public accessor exists. Friends are not deprecated — they are just a stronger coupling than necessary when public accessors suffice.
`a + b` = `Meter{30}`, then `Meter{30} + c` = `Meter{60}`. The result is a temporary, and `.v()` returns 60. Chaining works because each call returns a new value.
